items.prompt: The three coordinate planes (XY, YZ and ZX) divide all of space into how many octants?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - maths-ch11 Sec 11.2 Coordinate axes and planes / Exercise 11.1 Q4(iii)

items.prompt: A point lies on the x-axis. What can you say about its y- and z-coordinates?
items.answer: Both its y-coordinate and z-coordinate are zero

expected.correct: Both its y-coordinate and z-coordinate are zero
options: Both its y-coordinate and z-coordinate are zero
Both its x-coordinate and y-coordinate are zero

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - maths-ch11 Sec 11.3 Note / Exercise 11.1 Q1

items.prompt: In which octant does the point (-3, 1, -2) lie? (Use the sign table; answer with the octant number in Roman numerals.)
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - maths-ch11 Sec 11.3 Remark, Table 11.1 / Example 2

items.prompt: Find the distance between the points (2, 3, 5) and (4, 3, 1). Give the answer as a decimal (units).
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - maths-ch11 Sec 11.4 Distance formula / Exercise 11.2 Q1(i)

items.prompt: Find the distance of the point (1, 2, 2) from the origin, using OQ = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2). Give the numerical value (units).
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - maths-ch11 Sec 11.4 Distance from the origin

items.prompt: For the points P(-2, 3, 5), Q(1, 2, 3) and R(7, 0, -1) the distances are PQ = sqrt(14), QR = 2 sqrt(14) and PR = 3 sqrt(14). Show, using these distances, that P, Q and R are collinear, and state the distance condition you used.

expected.rubric: Three points are collinear if the largest pairwise distance equals the sum of the other two. Here PQ + QR = sqrt(14) + 2 sqrt(14) = 3 sqrt(14) = PR, so the condition PQ + QR = PR holds and therefore P, Q and R are collinear (Q lies between P and R on the line).
markPoints: States the collinearity condition: the largest distance equals the sum of the other two
Computes PQ + QR = sqrt(14) + 2 sqrt(14) = 3 sqrt(14)
Concludes PQ + QR = PR, hence the points are collinear

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - maths-ch11 Sec 11.4 Example 4 / Exercise 11.2 Q2

items.prompt: For A(3, 6, 9), B(10, 20, 30), C(25, -41, 5) the squared side lengths are AB^2 = 686, BC^2 = 4571, CA^2 = 2709. Is triangle ABC right-angled?
items.answer: No, because no side's square equals the sum of the squares of the other two

expected.correct: No, because no side's square equals the sum of the squares of the other two
options: No, because no side's square equals the sum of the squares of the other two
Yes, because all three sides are equal
Yes, because two sides are equal

items.prompt: Let P(x, y, z) be equidistant from A(3, 4, -5) and B(-2, 1, 4). Set PA = PB, square both sides, expand and simplify to obtain the equation of the set of points P.

expected.rubric: From PA = PB, square to get (x-3)^2 + (y-4)^2 + (z+5)^2 = (x+2)^2 + (y-1)^2 + (z-4)^2. Expanding and cancelling the x^2, y^2, z^2 terms leaves the linear equation 10x + 6y - 18z - 29 = 0, which is the required set of points (a plane, the perpendicular bisector of AB).
markPoints: Sets PA = PB and squares to remove the square roots

items.prompt: The centroid of triangle ABC is at (1, 1, 1). If A is (3, -5, 7) and B is (-1, 7, -6), find the z-coordinate of vertex C. (Use that each centroid coordinate is the average of the three vertices' coordinates.)
A triangle has vertices A(0, 0, 6), B(0, 4, 0) and C(6, 0, 0). Find the length of the median drawn from A to the midpoint of BC.
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - maths-ch11 Miscellaneous Exercise Q2 (median length via distance formula)
